contents.value: **.data** — MIPS Assembly keyword.
**.text** — MIPS Assembly keyword.
**.globl** — MIPS Assembly keyword.
**.align** — MIPS Assembly keyword.
**.ascii** — MIPS Assembly keyword.
**add** — Built-in function/command in MIPS Assembly.
**addi** — Built-in function/command in MIPS Assembly.
**addiu** — Built-in function/command in MIPS Assembly.
**sub** — Built-in function/command in MIPS Assembly.
**subi** — Built-in function/command in MIPS Assembly.

A **register** is a small, fast storage location in the CPU.
```mips
$t0, $s0, $a0, $v0, $ra, $sp
```
An **instruction** is a single CPU operation (add, sub, lw, sw, beq, j).

A **label** marks a memory address for branching.
```mips
loop: ...
  j loop
```

**syscall** invokes an operating system service.
```mips
li $v0, 10  # exit
syscall
```

An **assembler directive** controls assembly but produces no code.
```mips
.data  .text  .word  .asciiz  .globl
```
The **stack** stores function call frames, local variables, and return addresses.

An **immediate** is a constant value encoded in the instruction.
```mips
addi $t0, $zero, 42
```

**Load/Store** instructions transfer data between registers and memory.
```mips
lw $t0, 0($sp)    sw $t0, 0($sp)
```

A **branch** instruction changes control flow based on a condition.
```mips
beq $t0, $t1, label
bne $t0, $zero, label
```

**Jump** instructions transfer control unconditionally.
```mips
j label      jal function      jr $ra
```
**Memory segments** organize program data (.text, .data, .stack, .heap).

A **word** is the natural data size (32 bits in MIPS).
```mips
.word 42
lw $t0, addr
```

A **byte** is 8 bits, used for characters and small data.
```mips
.byte 65
lb $t0, addr
```

**Addressing modes** determine how operands are accessed.
```mips
Base: lw $t0, 4($sp)
Immediate: addi $t0, $t0, 5
```

**Pseudo-instructions** are assembler shorthands expanded to real instructions.
```mips
li $t0, 100    move $t0, $t1    bgt $t0, $t1, label
```

```mips
mul $rd, $rs, $rt
```
Multiplies the values in registers rs and rt, storing the lower 32 bits of the result in register rd. A pseudo-instruction in some assemblers that wraps mult and mflo.

```mips
and $rd, $rs, $rt
```
Performs a bitwise AND operation between the values in registers rs and rt, storing the result in register rd for masking and flag extraction operations.

```mips
or $rd, $rs, $rt
```
Performs a bitwise OR operation between the values in registers rs and rt, storing the result in register rd for combining bit flags or setting specific bits.

```mips
sll $rd, $rt, shamt
```
Shifts the value in register rt left by the specified shift amount of 0 to 31 bits, filling vacated positions with zeros and storing the result in register rd.

```mips
srl $rd, $rt, shamt
```
Shifts the value in register rt right by the specified shift amount of 0 to 31 bits, filling vacated positions with zeros as a logical shift and storing result in register rd.

```mips
lw $rt, offset($rs)
```
Loads a 32-bit word from memory at the address computed by adding the sign-extended offset to register rs, storing the value in register rt. Address must be word-aligned.

```mips
sw $rt, offset($rs)
```
Stores the 32-bit word from register rt into memory at the address computed by adding the sign-extended offset to register rs. Address must be word-aligned.

```mips
lb $rt, offset($rs)
```
Loads a byte from memory at the computed address, sign-extends it to 32 bits, and stores the result in register rt. Used for reading signed character data.

```mips
sb $rt, offset($rs)
```
Stores the least significant byte of register rt into memory at the address computed by adding the sign-extended offset to register rs.

```mips
la $rt, label
```
Pseudo-instruction that loads the 32-bit address of a label into register rt. Typically expands to a lui and ori instruction pair for the upper and lower 16 bits.

```mips
li $rt, immediate
```
Pseudo-instruction that loads a 32-bit immediate constant into register rt, expanding to lui and ori as needed depending on the magnitude of the value.

```mips
beq $rs, $rt, label
```
Branch on Equal compares registers rs and rt, and if they are equal, branches to the target label by adding the sign-extended offset to the program counter.

```mips
bne $rs, $rt, label
```
Branch on Not Equal compares registers rs and rt, and if they differ, branches to the target label by adding the sign-extended offset to the program counter.

```mips
slt $rd, $rs, $rt
```
Set on Less Than sets register rd to 1 if the signed value in rs is less than the signed value in rt, otherwise sets rd to 0. Essential for comparison-based branching.

```mips
j target
```
Unconditional Jump instruction that jumps to the instruction at the specified 26-bit target address, with the upper 4 bits from the current program counter. Used for loops and gotos.

```mips
jal target
```
Jump and Link saves the return address PC+4 in register $ra and jumps to the target address. The standard instruction for calling subroutines in MIPS assembly programs.

```mips
jr $rs
```
Jump Register jumps to the address stored in register rs. Commonly used with $ra to return from a subroutine called by jal, restoring execution to the caller.

```mips
$v0 (Register 2)
```
Value register 0, used for function return values and syscall service numbers. By convention, integer return values from functions are placed in $v0 before returning.

```mips
$a0 (Register 4)
```
Argument register 0, used to pass the first argument to subroutines and syscalls. Part of the $a0-$a3 convention for the first four function parameters in MIPS calling convention.

```mips
$sp (Register 29)
```
Stack Pointer register, pointing to the top of the runtime stack in memory. Grows downward by convention; functions adjust it with addi $sp, $sp, -N to allocate stack frames.

```mips
$ra (Register 31)
```
Return Address register, automatically set by the jal instruction to hold the address of the instruction following the call. Used by jr $ra to return from subroutines.

```mips
.asciiz "string"
```
Assembler directive that stores a null-terminated ASCII string in the data segment, automatically appending a zero byte. Used for strings passed to print_string syscall.

```mips
.word value [, value ...]
```
Assembler directive that stores one or more 32-bit integer values in the data segment, each occupying four consecutive bytes with proper word alignment.
